summary.executiveSummary: AuditEscrow is a generic shared-object escrow holding a payer's funds for an audit job, settled via capture() (pay authority) or refund() (return payer). The cryptographic mechanics are sound (funds held as Balance, object consumed by value so no double-settlement), but the trust model is broken: the payer can unilaterally refund at any time, so the authority/treasury is never guaranteed payment, and settlement is decided entirely off-chain with no on-chain binding to an actual audit result. There is also a vestigial status field that is set but never enforced. No fund-loss-to-third-party or overflow bugs were found.

findings.title: Payer can unilaterally refund, defeating the payment guarantee (capture/refund race)
findings.description: Both capture() and refund() accept either the payer or the authority as caller, and neither checks any condition (such as a completed/failed audit result) before settling. Because the escrow is a shared object consumed by value, capture and refund are mutually exclusive and resolve by whichever transaction lands first. The payer always has the incentive and the unconditional ability to call refund() and pull their funds back, even after a successful audit has been delivered.
findings.impact: The authority (treasury) can never rely on being paid: after receiving the off-chain audit result the payer simply submits refund() and reclaims the funds, or front-runs/races the authority's capture(). The escrow provides no real guarantee of payment to the service provider — the core purpose of the contract is unenforced.
findings.recommendation: Enforce an asymmetric, condition-gated settlement. For example: only the authority may capture(), and refund() should only be permitted after a timeout/deadline or when the authority has not captured, or gate settlement on an attested audit-result/job-completion proof. Add a deadline field and an explicit state machine (LOCKED -> CAPTURED/REFUNDED) so the two paths cannot freely race.
findings.codeSnippet: assert!(sender == escrow.payer || sender == escrow.authority, ENotAuthorized); // identical guard on both capture and refund

findings.title: Settlement is fully off-chain-trusted with no binding to the audit job or result (replay/spoofing of AI outcome)
findings.description: The escrow has no field tying it to a specific audit job, model output, nonce, or result attestation. capture() pays the authority and refund() pays the payer purely on the basis of who signs, with no on-chain evidence that the audit actually completed (success or failure). The off-chain AI/audit result is the sole authority for which path is taken, and nothing on-chain prevents the same off-chain result from being reused to justify settling multiple distinct escrows (replay of an off-chain decision).
findings.impact: Track-specific risk: the trust boundary between the off-chain AI result and the on-chain action is unguarded. The authority can capture funds without any proof the audit was performed; the payer can refund despite a delivered result; and a single off-chain approval can be replayed across escrows since none are bound to a unique job id. Disputes are unresolvable on-chain.
findings.recommendation: Record an immutable job_id / result_hash in the escrow at lock() time and require a matching signed attestation (or job-id check) at capture()/refund(). Emit and verify a per-job nonce so an off-chain authorization cannot be replayed against other escrows. Consider an arbiter or two-phase commit where the result hash is posted before settlement.
findings.codeSnippet: let AuditEscrow { id, payer: _, authority, funds, amount, status: _ } = escrow; // no job/result binding consulted

findings.title: status field is set but never validated (dead state machine)
findings.description: STATUS_LOCKED is the only status constant and the status field is written at lock() but is explicitly ignored (status: _) in both capture() and refund(). There is no LOCKED -> CAPTURED/REFUNDED transition and no assertion that the escrow is in an expected state. The field gives a false impression of state-machine protection that does not exist.
findings.impact: No direct exploit because the object is consumed by value (so it cannot be settled twice), but the unused field is misleading and would not catch a future code path that retains the object. It also wastes storage gas.
findings.recommendation: Either remove the status field entirely (the by-value consumption already prevents re-settlement) or implement and assert a real state machine, including the missing CAPTURED/REFUNDED constants.
findings.codeSnippet: status: STATUS_LOCKED, // written, then always destructured as status: _

findings.title: lock() performs no validation of amount or authority
findings.description: lock() accepts a Coin<T> of any value (including zero) and an arbitrary authority address with no checks (e.g. authority != payer, authority != @0x0, amount > 0). A zero-value escrow can be created, and the authority can be set to the zero address or to the payer themselves.
findings.impact: Low: enables creation of meaningless/zero escrows (minor spam) and footgun configurations such as authority == @0x0 (funds capturable only to the zero address) or authority == payer (no real escrow). Not a direct theft vector.
findings.recommendation: Add assertions: amount > 0, authority != @0x0, and (depending on intended semantics) authority != payer, with dedicated error codes.
findings.codeSnippet: let amount = coin::value(&payment); // no assert!(amount > 0); authority unchecked

findings.title: Redundant cached amount field duplicates the live balance
findings.description: The struct stores both funds: Balance<T> and a cached amount: u64, while the amount() accessor reads balance::value(&funds) instead of the cached field. Since funds is never mutated after creation the two are always equal, making the cached amount and the unused status field pure storage overhead.
findings.impact: Minor extra storage gas per escrow and a latent inconsistency risk if partial-withdrawal logic is ever added without updating amount.
findings.recommendation: Drop the cached amount (and status) fields and derive amount from balance::value where needed, or document that they are immutable snapshots for event emission only.
findings.codeSnippet: amount: u64, // cached, while amount() reads balance::value(&escrow.funds)

expensivePatterns: Shared object creation per escrow (transfer::share_object) incurs ongoing storage and consensus cost for every audit job.
Storing redundant amount and unused status fields adds per-object storage gas.
optimizationSuggestions: Remove the unused status field and the redundant cached amount field to shrink object storage.

If escrows are short-lived single-settlement objects, consider whether an owned object (sent to authority/payer as an escrow holder) could replace a shared object to avoid consensus overhead, depending on the required signing flexibility.

overallRecommendations: Fix the settlement trust model (FIND-001): make capture/refund asymmetric and condition/deadline-gated so the payer cannot unilaterally reclaim funds after a delivered audit and the two paths cannot freely race.
Bind each escrow to a unique audit job id and a result attestation, with a nonce to prevent replay of off-chain AI decisions across escrows (FIND-002).
Introduce a real state machine with explicit CAPTURED/REFUNDED states and assertions, or remove the misleading status field (FIND-003).
Add input validation in lock(): amount > 0, authority != @0x0, and authority != payer as appropriate (FIND-004).
Remove redundant cached fields to reduce storage gas and eliminate latent inconsistency (FIND-005).
